tools.name: batch_design
tools.description:
Execute multiple insert/copy/update/replace/move/delete/image operations in a single call.

## Usage

- For larger designs, split work into multiple batch_design calls by logical sections (e.g., screen structure first, then sidebar content, then main content).
- Avoid creating large operation objects like an insert with multiple descendants. Prefer breaking it down into many separate operations instead.
- If one of the operations fails, all previously executed operations in that block will be rolled back.
- Use unique binding names — defining a binding with the same name as an existing one overwrites it.
- A list of potential issues will be returned in the response message. Try to fix them in the next batch_design call.

## API

### Operation list format

You can create a list of operations in the following syntax:

- Every single operation must follow the Javascript syntax described below.
- ONLY these operation functions are supported.
- Every single operation line must be a single operation call with a possible binding assignment and nothing else.
- For node data, always follow the .pen schema.
- This parent node will be used for the operations inserted/copied/moved. For these operations defining a parent is REQUIRED.
- The "document" binding is a predefined binding and it references the root node of the document, ONLY use this, when creating screens and container frames. DO NOT create "document" as binding name.
- Don't include any comments or empty lines in the input.

### Insert (I)

Definition: insertedNodeId=I(parent: string, nodeData: Schema.Child)

- Important: "id"s are always created automatically for nodes, never create "id" properties in new node data.
- An insert can only be a single node, if you want to add children to it, use bindings and do it in a new Insert (I) operation.
- When working with components (reusable: true), insert their instances as refs with their properties overridden. If you want to override properties of subcomponents use subsequent Update (U) operations.
- Use the Replace (R) operation to override children inside a component instance, e.g. 'R("myInstance/childId",{type:"text",...})'
- Returns the inserted node ID as string

### Copy (C)

Definition: copiedNodeId=C(path: string, parent: string, copyNodeData: Schema.Child & { positionPadding?: number; positionDirection?: string })

- When copying a node and modifying its descendants, you MUST use the "descendants" property in the Copy operation itself. DO NOT use separate Update operations for descendants of copied nodes, as this will fail due to ID mismatches. The copied node and its descendants receive new IDs, so Update operations referencing the original descendant IDs will fail.
- "path": The ID of the existing node to copy. If you want to customize some properties of the copied node, just add them next to the 'path' property. If you want to customize nested nodes _under_ the copied one, use the same kind of 'descendants' map that 'ref' nodes use!
- "descendants": Optional, used for components. An object which keys are node IDs or paths to descendant objects inside the component used to customize the properties of descendant objects.
- Example of correct usage: 'label1=C("NKYzH",container,{descendants:{"ZopUS/jEYMs":{content:"First Name"}}})'
- "positionPadding": The minimum padding distance from other element when positioning if needed.
- "positionDirection": The direction to search for empty space relative to the node, to position the copied node if needed. Possible values are: "top", "right", "bottom", "left"
- Copying a reusable node creates a connected instance (a 'ref' node).
- Returns the copied node ID as string

### Update (U)

Definition: U(path: string, updateData: Schema.Child)

- Update the properties of existing nodes, without listing their children.
- Use this operation to create small incremental updates to the properties of existing nodes.
- DO NOT use this operation to update the node's "children", use Replace (R) operation for that.
- This operation CANNOT change the 'id', 'type' or 'ref' properties of any node!
- "path": The valid ID of the existing node to update (DO NOT use bindings in "path"), or if you want to update a nested node inside a component, use the ID of the nested node must be prefixed with the ID of the component instance and a slash (/). E.g. consider this component:
{"id":"button","type":"frame","reusable":true,"children":[{"id":"container","type":"frame","children":[{"id":"label","type":"text","content":"Button text"}]}]}
And then an instance of this component like this: {"id":"submit-button","type":"ref","ref":"button"}
The label text of 'submit-button' can be changed by passing the following as the "nodes" parameter:

``` javascript
U("submit-button/label",{content:"Submit"})
```

This slash-separated instance ID scheme works for any number of nesting levels, not just two.

- "updateData": The node data to update

### Replace (R)

Definition: replacedNodeId=R(path: string, nodeData: Schema.Child)

- Replace a node in the .pen file.
- This tool is ideal for swapping out parts of a component instance with new nodes.
- Important: "id"s are always created automatically for nodes, never create "id" properties in new node data.
- Returns the replaced node ID as string
- "path": The path of the node which will be replaced
- "nodeData": The properties of the new node

### Move (M)

Definition: M(nodeId: string, parent: string | undefined, index?: number)

- Move a nodes to a different location in the node tree in a .pen file.
- "path": The id of the moved node. ALWAYS use a valid node id, NOT a path or binding.
- "parent": Optional parent node Id or binding
- "index": Optional new position of the moved node among its siblings. If omitted, the node is placed at the end.

### Delete (D)

Definition: D(nodeId: string)

- Delete a node from a .pen file.
- "nodeId": The ID of the node to delete. ALWAYS use a valid node id, NOT a path or binding.

### Generate/Get Stock Image (G)

Definition: G(nodeId: string, type: "ai" | "stock", prompt: string)

- IMPORTANT: There is NO "image" node type! Images are applied as FILLS to frame/rectangle nodes.
- Do not generate random URLs for image fills, always use the G operation to get an image from a stock or AI service.
- To display an image: first Insert a frame or rectangle, then use G to apply the image as a fill.
- "nodeId": The ID of the frame/rectangle node to apply the image fill to. Can be a valid node ID or a binding name (e.g., "myFrame") created earlier in this operation list.
- "type": Either "ai" for AI-generated images or "stock" for stock photos from Unsplash.
- "prompt":
  - For "ai": a detailed descriptive prompt.
  - For "stock": 1-3 keyword search query following Stock query rules (simple, concrete, no use-case or abstract terms).

Examples:
- First create a frame, then apply an ai generated image:

heroImg=I("parentId",{type:"frame",name:"Hero Image",width:400,height:300})
G(heroImg,"ai","modern office workspace bright")

- AI-generated image on existing node:

G("logo-frame","ai","minimalist coffee shop logo, flat design")

## Property Compatibility

Applying a property a node type doesn't support fails validation and rolls back the whole batch. Rules:
- `children` and layout (`layout`, `gap`, `padding`, `justifyContent`, `alignItems`): only on `frand `group`. All other types are leaves — wrap in a frame to group them.
	- `fill`, `stroke`, `cornerRadius`: NOT on `group` (wrap in a frame instead).
	- `cornerRadius`: only on `frame`, `rectangle`, `polygon`. Not on ellipse, line, path, text, icon_font.
	- Text-only (`content`, `fontSize`, `fontFamily`, `fontWeight`, `textAlign`, etc.): only on `text`. `text` also requires `textGrowth` before `width`/`height` take effect.
	- Icon-only (`iconFontName`, `iconFontFamily`, `weight`): only on `icon_font`. Icon color goes in `fill` (icon_font has no `stroke`).
	- `ref`: customize children via `descendants` or follow-up `U`/`R` on `instanceId/childId` paths — do not put `children` directly on it."

## Key Points

- Every Insert (I), Copy (C) and Replace (R) operation MUST have a binding name. To reference a parent node in later operations, use a currently valid binding name from an earlier Insert, Copy or Replace operation.
- Not all bindings need to be used as parents later - it's okay to have "unused" bindings. The requirement exists to enforce deliberate structure.
- Operations execute sequentially; on error, all operations in the list will be rolled back.
- The 'placeholder:true' property marks a frame as a container for child content. Use that frame's ID as the parent target for operations that add children.

### Working with Existing Frames

- Use the frame's ID directly as the parent: `existingFrameId`
- If you update a frame's layout/placeholder properties, insert children INTO that same frame using its ID
- To insert into a slot (a non-unique node with overridden children), use the slot's canonicalized path: `instanceId/slotId`

### Using bindings for paths and node ids

- You can use a binding variable name instead of an inline string an operation call
- If you want to combine it with some other binding or a string use the "+" operator
- IMPORTANT: DO NOT try to Update (U) a node's descendant that you just copied (C), since copying will recreate the descendant nodes and it will assign new IDs to those children nodes.

``` javascript
foo=I("parentId",{type:"ref",ref:"caSd2fv"})
U(foo+"/Csawf3",{content:"+240%"})
D(foo)
```

## Examples

``` javascript
// When you insert a component instance and want to modify its descendants:

// 1. Update properties → Use U() with the instance path:
card=I(body,{type:"ref",ref:"CardComp"})
U(card+"/titleText",{content:"New Title"})

// 2. Replace a descendant entirely → R() with the instance path:
card=I(body,{type:"ref",ref:"CardComp"})
newTitle=R(card+"/headerSlot",{type:"text",content:"Custom Title"})

// 3. Add new children → Use I() on regular frames or document root:
container=I(body,{type:"frame",layout:"vertical"})
item=I(container,{type:"text",content:"New item"})

// Adding content to existing frame "MmNEt":
U("MmNEt",{placeholder:true,layout:"horizontal",gap:16})
content=I("MmNEt",{type:"frame",layout:"vertical"})
header=C("A2sa3f",content,{width:"fill_container",height:"fill_container"})

// Inserting into a slot inside a component instance "cardInstance" with slot "contentSlot" with a quote inside:
item=I("cardInstance/contentSlot",{type:"text",content:"'Example quote in slot content'"})

// Dashboard layout structure (6 ops)
// Prefer adding components directly into an existing frame.
sidebar=I("29c0s",{type:"ref",ref:"JRlf7",x:0,y:0,width:240,height:"fill_container"})
mainContent=I("29c0s",{type:"frame",layout:"vertical",gap:24,padding:32})
stats=I(mainContent,{type:"frame",layout:"vertical",gap:16})
card1=I(stats,{type:"ref",ref:"QMBKc",width:"fill_container",height:120})
U("FVge3x/vdS2egl",{width:"fill_container",height:"fill_container"})
U("FVge3x/gDsgE6S",{content:"Submit"})

// Form inputs section (4 ops)
// Add form inputs to an existing content area.
input1=I("mainContent",{type:"ref",ref:"FmgD2",width:"fill_container"})
U(input1+"/CvD2R",{content:"First Name"})
contact=R("FmgD2/vfRs3a",{ref:"1YtpE",type:"ref",width:"fill_container"})
U(contact+"/oknii",{content:"Full Name"})

// This example inserts a component directly into an existing frame.
sidebar=I("d3902",{type:"ref",ref:"JRlf7",x:0,y:0,width:240,height:"fill_container(500)"})

// Copying a non-reusable frame (eg duplicating a screen). This copies the dashboard created above and tweaks it as another variation:
dashboardV2=C("Xk9f2",document,{name:"Dashboard V2",positionDirection:"right",positionPadding:100})
D(dashboardV2+"/sidebar")
U(dashboardV2+"/stats/card1",{fill:"#E8F5E9"})
newContent=R(dashboardV2+"/stats/card3",{type:"text",content:"New Content"})

// To insert a component instance with their children overridden:
foo=I("pcCAt2f",{type:"ref",ref:"CMXky",children:[{type:"text",content:"0 m/h",fontSize:14}]})
```

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

input.description: JavaScript string snippet with a series of function calls to modify the design.

tools.name: batch_get
tools.description:
Retrieve nodes by searching for matching patterns and by node ids in batches.

- IMPORTANT: Combine multiple search patterns and node ID searches into one call.
- IMPORTANT: When you want to perform multiple searches and node id reads after another, combine them into one call.
- If neither patterns nor nodeIds is specified, returns the top-level children of the document.
- When using design system components, AVOID reading each component one-by-one. Instead list all of the available components in a single tool call.
- To list components of a design system, search for reusable nodes inside the design systems frame.
- Use this tool in the beginning to list components of a design system or design kit to understand its available components.
- This tool will return the searched nodes and its direct children. Use searchDepth to define how many levels to search in.
- Be careful using readDepth > 3, because it can return large amounts of data overflowing the context. Prefer using low numbers.
- Path geometry is abbreviated by default; set includePathGeometry=true for full geometry data
- Set resolveVariables=true to see computed values instead of variable references

Traversing large documents:
1. Start by reading top-level nodes, or nodes you already know the IDs of to understand overall structure
2. Decide if the nodes you have already read are sufficient for your task
2. If you need more data and you see "..." for children, make new read calls with those specific child IDs

## Example

To search for the reusable components in the design system:
{
  "filePath": "designs/forms.pen",
  "patterns": [{ "reusable": true }],
  "readDepth": 2,
  "searchDepth": 3
}

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

includePathGeometry.description: By default the `geometry` property of `path` nodes is replaced with "...". To see the path geometry in full, pass `true` for this flag.

nodeIds.description: Array of node IDs to read. Prefer reading multiple node IDs to read at once rather than separately.

parentId.description: Parent node ID to search within. If omitted, will search the whole document.

name.description: Only return nodes whose name matches this Regex pattern

reusable.description: Only return nodes with `reusable` equal to this value

type.description: Only return nodes with this type

readDepth.description: Limit how deep to descend in the node tree for a read node. If omitted, set to 1, meaning only the node itself is returned and its direct children. When maxDepth is reached, omitted children are replaced with "...".

resolveInstances.description: If you pass `true` for this flag, then `ref` nodes won't appear in the output; component instances will be fully expanded. This can be used to examine the full structure of a component instance. The `id`s of resolved shadow nodes will be their full ID paths, which can be used to customize them via update_properties.

resolveVariables.description: Node properties can be bound to variables, whose value depends on the theme settings of the node. If you want to see the currently visible values of such properties, pass `true` for this flag.

searchDepth.description: Limit how deep the search should descend in the node tree. If omitted, the search is unlimited in depth.

tools.name: export_nodes
tools.description:
Export nodes to image files in PNG/JPEG/WEBP/PDF formats.

- Takes an array of node IDs and exports each one as a separate image file.
- Supports PNG, JPEG, WEBP and PDF formats.
- Writes files to the specified output directory using node IDs as filenames.
- Returns the absolute paths of the created files.
- Uses 2x scale and 8192 max resolution for high-quality exports.
- For PDF, all nodes are combined into a single multi-page document.

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

nodeIds.description: Array of node IDs to export

outputDir.description: Directory to write exported files to

quality.description: Export quality for JPEG and WEBP (1-100). Defaults to 95 for JPEG, 100 (lossless) for WEBP.

scale.description: Export scale factor (default: 2). Not applicable to PDF.

tools.name: find_empty_space_on_canvas
tools.description:
Find empty space in a .pen file for a given direction and desired size.

If nodeId is provided, finds space around that specific node.
If nodeId is omitted, finds space around the entire canvas content.

direction.description: The direction to search for empty space relative to the node

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

height.description: The height of the required empty space

nodeId.description: Optional starting point node ID to find space around. If omitted, the empty space will be found around the canvas content.

padding.description: The minimum padding distance from other element

width.description: The width of the required empty space

tools.name: get_editor_state
tools.description: Get the currently active canvas editor, the current user selection and other essential design information to get started on a design task.

include_schema.description: Include the .pen file schema. Must be true before any read or write operations. Set to false only if the schema has already been loaded in this conversation.

tools.name: get_guidelines
tools.description:
Load guides and styles for working with .pen files.

- guide: Task-specific instructions that teach you how to perform a task correctly.
- style: Visual style archetypes with configurable fonts, colors, and imagery.

Usage:
1. get_guidelines(): list available guides and styles
2. get_guidelines({ category, name }): load guide with no params or get required params when required
3. get_guidelines({ category, name, params }): load guide or style

name.description: Guideline name from the category listing

params.description: Key-value pairs for required params returned in step 2

tools.name: get_screenshot
tools.description:
Returns a screenshot of a node in a .pen file.

Usage:
- Screenshots are expensive — use sparingly. Take one only after a complete section is done, not after every batch_design.
- Prefer the smallest meaningful node (a section frame, not the whole document) — large nodes use more tokens and obscure detail.
- Use snapshot_layout for structural/sizing checks; reach for a screenshot only when visual fidelity (color, type, alignment) is what you need to verify.

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

nodeId.description: ID of the node to take screenshot of

tools.name: get_variables
tools.description:
Get the variables and themes defined in this .pen file.

- Use the result of this tool to create global CSS rules to use in a codebase.
- When generating code from a design, use this tool to get the global styling rules associated with the design.

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

tools.name: open_document
tools.description:
Use this tool in the following two scenarios ONLY:
- if user requests to open a specific .pen file, pass the absolute file path to open it.
- if there is no active editor opened currently, omit path, and a new document will open.

path.description: Absolute path to an existing .pen file.

tools.name: replace_all_matching_properties
tools.description: Recursively replace all matching properties on the entire node tree on provided parent ids.

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

parents.description: IDs of all parent nodes to search

properties.description: List of replacements for each property.

tools.name: search_all_unique_properties
tools.description: Recursively search for all unique properties on the entire node tree on provided parent ids.

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

parents.description: IDs of all parent nodes to search

properties.description: List of properties to search for

tools.name: set_variables

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

replace.description: By default, the variables are merged into the document's existing variable definitions. If you want to completely replace the document's existing variable definitions, pass `true` for this flag.

variables.description: Follow the variable definition in the .pen file schema.

tools.name: snapshot_layout
tools.description:
Check the current layout structure of an .pen file.

- Be careful with omitting the maxDepth parameter, as large maxDepth values can result in a lot of data.
- If you're only interested in overlapping elements, clipped elements, etc. then use the problemsOnly parameter.

Example:
- maxDepth=0 means only the top level nodes layout will be returned. Useful for finding space for a new frame on the canvas.

filePath.description: An optional file path to access a .pen file.

maxDepth.description: Limit how deep to descend in the node tree. If omitted, the search is limited to the node and its direct children. When maxDepth is reached, omitted children are replaced with "...". Unlimited depth can result in a lot of data, prefer specifying a maxDepth.

parentId.description: Only return the layout rectangles in this node's subtree. If omitted returns the layout of the whole document.

problemsOnly.description: Only return nodes that have some kind of problem with their layout, e.g. they are clipped by their parents. If omitted, the value is `false`.

tools.name: execute_code
tools.description:
Executes JavaScript code in the Penpot plugin context.
IMPORTANT: Before using this tool, make sure you have read the 'Penpot High-Level Overview' and know which Penpot API functionality is necessary and how to use it.
You have access two main objects: `penpot` (the Penpot API, of type `Penpot`), `penpotUtils`, and `storage`.
`storage` is an object in which arbitrary data can be stored, simply by adding a new attribute; stored attributes can be referenced in future calls to this tool, so any intermediate results that could come in handy later should be stored in `storage` instead of just a fleeting variable; you can also store functions and thus build up a library).
Think of the code being executed as the body of a function: The tool call returns whatever you return in the applicable `return` statement, if any. You can return arbitrary JS objects; no need to apply JSON.stringify.
If an exception occurs, the exception's message will be returned to you.
Any output that you generate via the `console` object will be returned to you separately; so you may use it to track what your code is doing, but you should *only* do so only if there is an ACTUAL NEED for this! VERY IMPORTANT: Don't use logging prematurely! NEVER log the data you are returning, as you will otherwise receive it twice!
VERY IMPORTANT: In general, try a simple approach first, and only if it fails, try more complex code that involves handling different cases (in particular error cases) and that applies logging.

code.description: The JavaScript code to execute in the plugin context.

tools.name: high_level_overview
tools.description: Returns basic high-level instructions on the usage of Penpot-related tools and the Penpot API. If you have already read the 'Penpot High-Level Overview', you must not call this tool.

tools.name: penpot_api_info
tools.description: Retrieves Penpot API documentation for types and their members.Be sure to read the 'Penpot High-Level Overview' first.

tools.name: export_shape
tools.description: Exports a shape (or a shape's image fill) from the Penpot design to a PNG or SVG image, such that you can get an impression of what it looks like.

shapeId.description: Identifier of the shape to export. Special identifiers you can use: 'selection' (first shape currently selected by the user), 'page' (entire current page)

mode.description: The export mode: either 'shape' (full shape as it appears in the design, including descendants; the default) or 'fill' (export the raw image that is used as a fill for the shape; PNG format only)
